answer:
Build Role-Based Admin Panel with Policy-Driven Access — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A role-based admin panel tests authorization architecture (policy-driven access control beyond simple role checks), dynamic UI rendering (components shown/hidden based on permissions), audit logging (who accessed/modified what), and admin CRUD operations with proper authorization guards. The 45-minute MVP: admin dashboard with role-specific views (Admin sees all, Manager sees team, User sees self), user management CRUD, role assignment, and action audit log.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

This is directly derived from the HR Leave Management System's 3-tier RBAC: Employee, Manager, HR Admin. Manoj built the exact authorization middleware pattern needed here — Fastify preHandler hooks that check JWT claims for role and evaluate resource-level policies (e.g., manager can only see their department's data). The admin panel UI mirrors the CRM Dashboard's role-conditional rendering — Angular's *ngIf="user.role === 'admin'" showed admin-only controls like bulk operations and system settings.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Policy Engine + Admin API

// Policy definitions
const POLICIES = {
  'user:list': {
    admin: () => ({}),  // No filter — see all
    manager: (ctx) => ({ departmentId: ctx.user.departmentId }), // Team only
    viewer: (ctx) => ({ id: ctx.user.sub }) // Self only
  },
  'user:update_role': {
    admin: () => true,
    manager: () => false,
    viewer: () => false
  },
  'user:delete': {
    admin: () => true,
    manager: () => false,
    viewer: () => false
  },
  'audit:read': {
    admin: () => ({}),
    manager: (ctx) => ({ targetDepartmentId: ctx.user.departmentId }),
    viewer: () => false
  }
};

// Policy evaluation middleware
function policyGuard(action) {
  return async (req, reply) => {
    const policy = POLICIES[action]?.[req.user.role];
    if (!policy) return reply.status(403).send({ error: 'No policy defined' });
    
    const result = policy({ user: req.user });
    if (result === false) return reply.status(403).send({ error: 'Action not permitted' });
    
    // Attach filter to request for data-level authorization
    req.policyFilter = result === true ? {} : result;
  };
}

// Audit logger
async function auditLog(actor, action, target, details = {}) {
  await prisma.auditLog.create({
    data: {
      actorId: actor.id,
      actorRole: actor.role,
      action,
      targetId: target?.id || null,
      targetType: target?.type || null,
      details: JSON.stringify(details),
      ip: actor.ip,
      timestamp: new Date()
    }
  });
}

// Admin API endpoints
app.get('/admin/users', {
  preHandler: [authenticate, policyGuard('user:list')]
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const users = await prisma.user.findMany({
    where: req.policyFilter,
    select: { id: true, name: true, email: true, role: true, departmentId: true, createdAt: true },
    orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }
  });
  return reply.send(users);
});

app.patch('/admin/users/:id/role', {
  preHandler: [authenticate, policyGuard('user:update_role')]
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const { role } = req.body;
  const targetUser = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
  
  if (!targetUser) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'User not found' });
  
  // Prevent self-demotion (admin can't remove their own admin role)
  if (req.params.id === req.user.sub && role !== 'admin') {
    return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Cannot change your own role' });
  }
  
  const updated = await prisma.user.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { role }
  });
  
  await auditLog(
    { id: req.user.sub, role: req.user.role, ip: req.ip },
    'role_change',
    { id: targetUser.id, type: 'user' },
    { oldRole: targetUser.role, newRole: role }
  );
  
  // Invalidate user's cached permissions
  await redis.del(`permissions:${req.params.id}`);
  await redis.sadd('force-refresh', req.params.id);
  
  return reply.send(updated);
});

app.get('/admin/audit-log', {
  preHandler: [authenticate, policyGuard('audit:read')]
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const logs = await prisma.auditLog.findMany({
    where: req.policyFilter,
    orderBy: { timestamp: 'desc' },
    take: 100,
    include: { actor: { select: { name: true } } }
  });
  return reply.send(logs);
});

// Frontend: Permission-based UI rendering
function AdminPanel() {
  const { user } = useAuth();
  const { data: users } = useQuery(['admin-users'], () =>
    fetch('/admin/users').then(r => r.json())
  );
  const { data: auditLog } = useQuery(['audit-log'], () =>
    fetch('/admin/audit-log').then(r => r.json()),
    { enabled: ['admin', 'manager'].includes(user.role) }
  );
  
  const permissions = {
    canChangeRole: user.role === 'admin',
    canDeleteUser: user.role === 'admin',
    canViewAudit: ['admin', 'manager'].includes(user.role)
  };
  
  return (
    

      
Admin Panel

      
          {permissions.canChangeRole && }
        {users?.map(u => (
          
            {permissions.canChangeRole && }
          
        ))}

        
NameEmailRoleActions

        

            {u.name}{u.email}
            {u.role}
              
            

      

      
      {permissions.canViewAudit && (
        

          
Audit Log

          
    {auditLog?.map(log => (
            
  • 
              {log.actor.name} — {log.action} — {new Date(log.timestamp).toLocaleString()}
              
    {JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(log.details), null, 2)}
    
            
    • 
          ))}

        

      )}
    

  );
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the HR Leave System's RBAC, Manoj learned three critical lessons: (1) Never trust frontend-only authorization — always enforce on the backend. The HR system initially hid the "Delete" button for non-admins but didn't guard the API endpoint, so a curl request could delete any user. (2) Policy filter pattern — instead of checking permission and fetching data separately, embed the authorization constraint in the database query itself. Manager queries automatically filter to their department. (3) Self-demotion guard — the last admin accidentally removed their own admin role, locking everyone out. He added a check preventing self-role-change.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Frontend-only authorizationAPI endpoints accessible via curl/PostmanBackend policyGuard middleware on every admin endpoint
Admin self-demotionLast admin loses access — system lockedPrevent changing own role; require another admin for demotion
No audit trailCan't trace who changed whatAudit log on every mutation with actor, action, old/new values
Role checks scattered in codeInconsistent authorization — some endpoints unguardedCentralized policy definitions + middleware pattern
Stale permissions after role changeUser retains old access until token expiresInvalidate cache + force-refresh on role change

Validation Strategy

  • Permission matrix test: Each role × each endpoint — verify expected 200 or 403
  • Data isolation: Manager queries → verify only team members returned (not other departments)
  • Self-demotion guard: Admin tries to change own role → verify rejected
  • Audit completeness: Every admin action (create, update, delete, role change) → verify audit log entry
  • Frontend rendering: Login as viewer → verify no admin controls visible

Create Resilient Webhook Ingestion Service with Retries — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A resilient webhook ingestion service tests security (HMAC signature verification), reliability patterns (idempotent processing, retry with backoff), observability (delivery tracking, dead-letter queue), and performance (acknowledge fast, process async). The 45-minute MVP: receive webhooks with signature verification, deduplicate events, queue for async processing, track delivery status, and handle failures with retry + DLQ.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

From the HR Leave System, Manoj built webhook handlers for email delivery status callbacks and from ContactsHub, Cloudinary upload completion webhooks. Both implementations evolved from synchronous processing (blocking the response) to the acknowledge-and-enqueue pattern after encountering provider timeouts. The retry logic mirrors the HR system's background job retry pattern with BullMQ — exponential backoff with max attempts. The HMAC signature verification reuses the same crypto.timingSafeEqual pattern from the HR system's JWT implementation.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Resilient Webhook Handler

// Complete webhook ingestion service
import { createHmac, timingSafeEqual } from 'crypto';
import { Queue, Worker } from 'bullmq';

const webhookQueue = new Queue('webhooks', { connection: { host: 'localhost' } });

// 1. Signature verification middleware
function verifySignature(secret) {
  return (req, reply, done) => {
    const sig = req.headers['x-webhook-signature'];
    const ts = req.headers['x-webhook-timestamp'];
    if (!sig || !ts) return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Missing signature' });
    
    // Replay protection: reject events older than 5 min
    if (Date.now() - parseInt(ts) > 300000) {
      return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Event too old' });
    }
    
    const expected = createHmac('sha256', secret)
      .update(`${ts}.${JSON.stringify(req.body)}`)
      .digest('hex');
    
    if (!timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(sig), Buffer.from(expected))) {
      metrics.increment('webhook.signature_invalid');
      return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid signature' });
    }
    done();
  };
}

// 2. Ingestion endpoint — acknowledge fast
app.post('/webhooks/ingest', {
  preHandler: verifySignature(process.env.WEBHOOK_SECRET)
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const start = performance.now();
  const eventId = req.headers['x-event-id'] || crypto.randomUUID();
  
  // Deduplicate
  const isNew = await redis.sadd('webhook:seen', eventId);
  if (!isNew) {
    metrics.increment('webhook.duplicate');
    return reply.send({ status: 'duplicate' });
  }
  await redis.expire('webhook:seen', 86400);
  
  // Store raw event for audit trail
  await prisma.webhookEvent.create({
    data: {
      id: eventId,
      payload: JSON.stringify(req.body),
      headers: JSON.stringify({
        signature: req.headers['x-webhook-signature'],
        timestamp: req.headers['x-webhook-timestamp']
      }),
      ip: req.ip,
      status: 'received'
    }
  });
  
  // Enqueue for async processing
  await webhookQueue.add(req.body.type || 'unknown', {
    eventId,
    payload: req.body
  }, {
    attempts: 3,
    backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 1000 }
  });
  
  metrics.histogram('webhook.ingestion_ms', performance.now() - start);
  return reply.send({ status: 'accepted', eventId });
});

// 3. Async processor with idempotent handlers
const worker = new Worker('webhooks', async (job) => {
  const { eventId, payload } = job.data;
  
  try {
    // Route by event type
    switch (payload.type) {
      case 'payment.completed':
        await prisma.payment.upsert({
          where: { externalId: payload.id },
          create: { externalId: payload.id, amount: payload.amount, status: 'completed' },
          update: { status: 'completed' }  // Idempotent
        });
        break;
      
      case 'user.updated':
        await prisma.user.update({
          where: { externalId: payload.userId },
          data: { email: payload.email, name: payload.name }
        });
        break;
      
      default:
        job.log(`Unknown event type: ${payload.type}`);
    }
    
    // Mark as processed
    await prisma.webhookEvent.update({
      where: { id: eventId },
      data: { status: 'processed', processedAt: new Date() }
    });
  } catch (err) {
    await prisma.webhookEvent.update({
      where: { id: eventId },
      data: { status: 'failed', error: err.message }
    });
    throw err;  // BullMQ retries
  }
}, {
  connection: { host: 'localhost' },
  concurrency: 5
});

// DLQ handler
worker.on('failed', async (job, err) => {
  if (job.attemptsMade >= job.opts.attempts) {
    metrics.increment('webhook.dlq');
    await prisma.webhookEvent.update({
      where: { id: job.data.eventId },
      data: { status: 'dead_letter', error: err.message }
    });
  }
});

// 4. Replay endpoint for failed events
app.post('/admin/webhooks/:eventId/replay', {
  preHandler: [authenticate, policyGuard('webhook:replay')]
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const event = await prisma.webhookEvent.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.eventId } });
  if (!event) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'Not found' });
  
  await webhookQueue.add('replay', {
    eventId: event.id,
    payload: JSON.parse(event.payload),
    isReplay: true
  });
  
  await auditLog(req.user, 'webhook_replay', { id: event.id, type: 'webhook' });
  return reply.send({ status: 'replayed' });
});

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From ContactsHub's Cloudinary webhook integration, Manoj learned that timing-safe comparison is critical for HMAC verification — regular string comparison (===) is vulnerable to timing attacks where an attacker can determine the correct signature byte by byte by measuring response times. He also learned from the HR system that upsert is the key to idempotent webhook processing — if the same event arrives twice, the second upsert updates with the same data (no-op), rather than throwing a duplicate key error. The event store pattern enables replay — critical for debugging production issues where a webhook was received but processing failed.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
String comparison for HMACTiming attack reveals correct signaturecrypto.timingSafeEqual for constant-time comparison
Synchronous processingProvider timeout → retries → duplicate stormRespond 200 in < 50ms → async queue processing
No deduplicationSame event processed multiple timesRedis SADD (atomic) with 24h TTL
No event storeCan't debug or replay failed eventsStore every raw event with headers, IP, payload
Immediate retry on failureTransient errors fail all retries instantlyExponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s between retries

Validation Strategy

  • Signature verification: Send event with wrong signature → verify 401
  • Replay protection: Send event with 10-min-old timestamp → verify rejection
  • Deduplication: Send same event 3 times → verify only 1 processing
  • Retry behavior: Force handler to fail → verify 3 retries with exponential backoff
  • DLQ + replay: Event fails 3 times → verify in DLQ → replay → verify successful processing

Design a Payments Reconciliation Report Pipeline — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A payments reconciliation pipeline tests data pipeline design (extract from multiple sources, transform/compare, load report), financial accuracy (cent-level precision, no floating-point errors), batch processing (large datasets processed efficiently), and reporting (discrepancy identification, summary statistics). The 45-minute MVP: ingest payment records from two sources (internal DB + payment provider), compare them, identify discrepancies (missing, amount mismatch, status mismatch), and generate a reconciliation report.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

From the CRM Dashboard's reporting features, Manoj built data aggregation pipelines using MongoDB's aggregation framework — grouping sales by period, calculating totals, and generating visual reports with Recharts. The reconciliation pattern extends this: instead of aggregating from one source, he compares two datasets. From ContactsHub's CSV import pipeline, he has the streaming parser and batch processing patterns needed for large payment files. The financial precision requirement maps to lessons from the CRM where sales totals showed rounding errors — he switched from Number to integer cents (amount * 100) to avoid floating-point issues.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Reconciliation Engine

// Payment reconciliation pipeline

// All amounts stored as integer cents (avoid floating-point)
// $10.50 = 1050 cents

async function reconcile(dateRange) {
  const { startDate, endDate } = dateRange;
  
  // 1. Extract: Load internal payments
  const internalPayments = await prisma.payment.findMany({
    where: {
      createdAt: { gte: new Date(startDate), lte: new Date(endDate) },
      status: { in: ['completed', 'refunded'] }
    }
  });
  
  // 2. Extract: Load external records (from provider API/CSV)
  const externalRecords = await fetchProviderRecords(startDate, endDate);
  
  // 3. Build lookup maps
  const internalMap = new Map(internalPayments.map(p => [p.externalId, p]));
  const externalMap = new Map(externalRecords.map(r => [r.transactionId, r]));
  
  // 4. Compare and categorize
  const result = {
    matched: [],
    amountMismatch: [],
    statusMismatch: [],
    missingFromProvider: [], // In our DB but not in provider
    missingFromInternal: [], // In provider but not in our DB
    summary: { totalInternal: 0, totalExternal: 0, variance: 0 }
  };
  
  // Check each internal payment against external
  for (const [extId, internal] of internalMap) {
    const external = externalMap.get(extId);
    
    if (!external) {
      result.missingFromProvider.push({
        internalId: internal.id, externalId: extId,
        amount: internal.amountCents, status: internal.status,
        issue: 'Payment exists internally but not found in provider'
      });
      continue;
    }
    
    // Compare amounts (integer cents — exact comparison)
    if (internal.amountCents !== external.amountCents) {
      result.amountMismatch.push({
        externalId: extId,
        internalAmount: internal.amountCents,
        externalAmount: external.amountCents,
        difference: internal.amountCents - external.amountCents,
        issue: `Amount differs by ${Math.abs(internal.amountCents - external.amountCents)} cents`
      });
      continue;
    }
    
    // Compare status
    const statusMap = { 'succeeded': 'completed', 'refunded': 'refunded' };
    if (statusMap[external.status] !== internal.status) {
      result.statusMismatch.push({
        externalId: extId,
        internalStatus: internal.status,
        externalStatus: external.status
      });
      continue;
    }
    
    result.matched.push({ externalId: extId, amount: internal.amountCents });
  }
  
  // Check for records in provider but not in our DB
  for (const [txnId, external] of externalMap) {
    if (!internalMap.has(txnId)) {
      result.missingFromInternal.push({
        externalId: txnId,
        amount: external.amountCents,
        status: external.status,
        issue: 'Payment in provider but missing from internal DB (missed webhook?)'
      });
    }
  }
  
  // Calculate summary
  result.summary = {
    totalRecords: internalPayments.length + result.missingFromInternal.length,
    matched: result.matched.length,
    matchRate: ((result.matched.length / internalPayments.length) * 100).toFixed(1) + '%',
    discrepancies: result.amountMismatch.length + result.statusMismatch.length,
    missingFromProvider: result.missingFromProvider.length,
    missingFromInternal: result.missingFromInternal.length,
    totalInternalCents: internalPayments.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.amountCents, 0),
    totalExternalCents: externalRecords.reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.amountCents, 0),
    varianceCents: internalPayments.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.amountCents, 0) -
      externalRecords.reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.amountCents, 0)
  };
  
  return result;
}

// API endpoint
app.post('/api/reconciliation/run', {
  preHandler: [authenticate, policyGuard('reports:generate')]
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const { startDate, endDate } = req.body;
  
  // Run as background job for large date ranges
  const daySpan = (new Date(endDate) - new Date(startDate)) / 86400000;
  
  if (daySpan > 7) {
    const job = await reportQueue.add('reconciliation', { startDate, endDate, userId: req.user.sub });
    return reply.status(202).send({ jobId: job.id, statusUrl: `/api/jobs/${job.id}/status` });
  }
  
  const result = await reconcile({ startDate, endDate });
  
  // Persist report
  await prisma.reconciliationReport.create({
    data: {
      dateRange: { startDate, endDate },
      summary: result.summary,
      discrepancies: JSON.stringify([
        ...result.amountMismatch,
        ...result.statusMismatch,
        ...result.missingFromProvider,
        ...result.missingFromInternal
      ]),
      generatedBy: req.user.sub
    }
  });
  
  return reply.send(result);
});

// Format currency from cents
function formatCents(cents) {
  return `$${(cents / 100).toFixed(2)}`;
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the CRM Dashboard, Manoj learned the floating-point money lesson the hard way: summing 1000 sales amounts in JavaScript produced $45,231.679999999993 instead of $45,231.68. The fix was storing all amounts as integer cents in the database and only converting to dollars for display. This is critical for reconciliation — a 1-cent rounding difference across 10,000 transactions could show a $100 discrepancy that doesn't actually exist. He also learned from the CRM's report generation that date range queries must use UTC consistently — a report for "January 2025" in IST vs UTC can include/exclude transactions at the boundary.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Floating-point amountsPhantom discrepancies from roundingInteger cents for all calculations; convert to dollars only for display
Timezone mismatch between sourcesTransactions at boundary appear missingNormalize all timestamps to UTC before comparison
Missing webhook = missing internal recordRevenue discrepancy goes unnoticedReconciliation catches these as "missing from internal" — trigger investigation
Large date range = slow queryTimeout on month-long reconciliationBackground job for ranges > 7 days; batch process by day
No report persistenceCan't audit past reconciliation resultsStore every report with generator, timestamp, and full results

Validation Strategy

  • 100% match test: Identical internal and external data → verify 100% match rate, zero discrepancies
  • Amount mismatch detection: Internal $10.00, external $10.50 → verify flagged with 50-cent difference
  • Missing record detection: Remove 1 internal record → verify appears in missingFromProvider
  • Integer precision: Sum 10,000 amounts → verify exact total (no rounding errors)
  • Large dataset performance: 100K records → verify completes within 30 seconds

Build a Feature Toggle Platform with Staged Rollout — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A feature toggle platform with staged rollout tests distributed configuration management (flags evaluated across multiple services), percentage-based targeting (deterministic user bucketing), staged release strategies (canary → gradual → full), and real-time flag propagation (changes take effect without redeployment). The 45-minute MVP: create feature flags with targeting rules, percentage rollout with deterministic hashing, staged rollout (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%), real-time flag updates via SSE, and a management dashboard.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj used feature flags to roll out Socket.io notifications progressively — starting with the engineering department, then expanding to 50%, then 100%. The deterministic hashing lesson (Math.random fails — same user gets different values on each request) directly applies here. From the CRM Dashboard, he tested a new chart library behind a NEW_CHARTS flag with 3 beta testers. The management dashboard UI reuses patterns from the ContactsHub admin settings page — toggle switches, dropdowns, and real-time feedback.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Feature Toggle Platform

// Complete feature toggle platform

// 1. Flag evaluation engine with deterministic hashing
class TogglePlatform {
  constructor(prisma, redis) {
    this.prisma = prisma;
    this.redis = redis;
    this.cache = new Map();
    this.sseClients = new Set();
    this.listenForUpdates();
  }
  
  // Deterministic hash — same user always gets same bucket
  hash(userId, flagKey) {
    const str = `${userId}:${flagKey}`;
    let hash = 5381;
    for (let i = 0; i < str.length; i++) {
      hash = ((hash << 5) + hash) + str.charCodeAt(i);
    }
    return Math.abs(hash) % 100;
  }
  
  async evaluate(flagKey, context = {}) {
    const flag = await this.getFlag(flagKey);
    if (!flag || !flag.enabled) return false;
    
    // Check targeting rules first
    if (flag.targeting) {
      if (flag.targeting.userIds?.includes(context.userId)) return true;
      if (flag.targeting.roles?.includes(context.role)) return true;
      if (flag.targeting.departments?.includes(context.department)) return true;
    }
    
    // Percentage rollout with deterministic hash
    if (flag.rolloutPct > 0 && context.userId) {
      return this.hash(context.userId, flagKey) < flag.rolloutPct;
    }
    
    // No targeting match and no rollout = disabled
    return !!flag.targeting; // If targeting exists but user doesn't match = false
  }
  
  async evaluateAll(context) {
    const flags = await this.prisma.featureFlag.findMany();
    const results = {};
    for (const flag of flags) {
      this.cache.set(flag.key, { data: flag, ts: Date.now() });
      results[flag.key] = await this.evaluate(flag.key, context);
    }
    return results;
  }
  
  async getFlag(key) {
    const cached = this.cache.get(key);
    if (cached && Date.now() - cached.ts < 30000) return cached.data;
    
    const redisVal = await this.redis.get(`flag:${key}`);
    if (redisVal) {
      const flag = JSON.parse(redisVal);
      this.cache.set(key, { data: flag, ts: Date.now() });
      return flag;
    }
    
    const flag = await this.prisma.featureFlag.findUnique({ where: { key } });
    if (flag) {
      await this.redis.setex(`flag:${key}`, 300, JSON.stringify(flag));
      this.cache.set(key, { data: flag, ts: Date.now() });
    }
    return flag;
  }
  
  // Real-time updates via Redis Pub/Sub
  listenForUpdates() {
    const sub = this.redis.duplicate();
    sub.subscribe('flag-updates');
    sub.on('message', (channel, message) => {
      const update = JSON.parse(message);
      this.cache.set(update.key, { data: update.flag, ts: Date.now() });
      // Notify SSE clients
      for (const client of this.sseClients) {
        client.write(`data: ${message}\n\n`);
      }
    });
  }
}

// 2. API endpoints
const platform = new TogglePlatform(prisma, redis);

app.post('/api/flags', async (req, reply) => {
  const { key, description, targeting, rolloutPct = 0 } = req.body;
  
  const flag = await prisma.featureFlag.create({
    data: { key, description, enabled: true, rolloutPct, targeting: targeting || null }
  });
  
  return reply.status(201).send(flag);
});

// Staged rollout endpoint
app.patch('/api/flags/:key/rollout', async (req, reply) => {
  const { key } = req.params;
  const { percentage } = req.body;
  const STAGES = [1, 10, 50, 100];
  
  const flag = await prisma.featureFlag.findUnique({ where: { key } });
  if (!flag) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'Flag not found' });
  
  // Validate staged progression
  const currentStage = STAGES.findIndex(s => s >= flag.rolloutPct);
  const targetStage = STAGES.findIndex(s => s >= percentage);
  
  if (targetStage > currentStage + 1 && percentage !== 0) {
    return reply.status(400).send({
      error: `Cannot skip stages. Current: ${flag.rolloutPct}%. Next allowed: ${STAGES[currentStage + 1]}% or rollback to 0%`
    });
  }
  
  const updated = await prisma.featureFlag.update({
    where: { key },
    data: { rolloutPct: percentage }
  });
  
  // Propagate immediately
  await redis.del(`flag:${key}`);
  await redis.publish('flag-updates', JSON.stringify({ key, flag: updated }));
  
  // Audit
  await prisma.flagAudit.create({
    data: {
      flagKey: key, action: 'rollout_change',
      oldValue: flag.rolloutPct.toString(),
      newValue: percentage.toString(),
      actorId: req.user.sub
    }
  });
  
  return reply.send({ key, rolloutPct: percentage, previousPct: flag.rolloutPct });
});

// Bulk evaluate for a user (used by frontend SDK)
app.get('/api/flags/evaluate', async (req, reply) => {
  const context = {
    userId: req.user.sub,
    role: req.user.role,
    department: req.user.departmentId
  };
  const flags = await platform.evaluateAll(context);
  return reply.send(flags);
});

// SSE stream for real-time flag updates
app.get('/api/flags/stream', (req, reply) => {
  reply.raw.writeHead(200, {
    'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream',
    'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
    'Connection': 'keep-alive'
  });
  
  platform.sseClients.add(reply.raw);
  req.raw.on('close', () => platform.sseClients.delete(reply.raw));
});

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the HR Leave System's feature flag experience, Manoj learned three critical design decisions: (1) Deterministic hashing — the hash must depend on both userId AND flagKey, not just userId. Otherwise, a user who's in the 10% bucket for flag A would also be in the 10% for flag B, creating correlated rollouts. Adding the flag key as salt distributes users independently across flags. (2) Staged rollout with gates — jumping from 1% to 100% is dangerous. Enforcing stages (1→10→50→100) gives time to monitor error rates between each promotion. (3) Rollback must be instant — setting rollout to 0% should propagate within seconds. The SSE + Redis Pub/Sub pattern achieves this.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Non-deterministic rollout hashUser experience flickers between enabled/disabledHash(userId + flagKey) — consistent result every time
Correlated rollout across flagsSame 10% gets ALL new featuresInclude flagKey in hash salt — independent distribution
Skipping rollout stagesBug affects 100% of users instantlyEnforce staged progression: 1→10→50→100 with mandatory monitoring
Slow flag propagationKill switch takes minutesSSE + Redis Pub/Sub → propagation in < 3 seconds
No audit trailCan't trace who enabled a problematic flagEvery flag change logged with actor, old/new value, timestamp

Validation Strategy

  • Deterministic test: Same userId evaluated 100 times → verify 100% consistent result
  • Independence test: 10% rollout on 2 flags → verify different user sets (not correlated)
  • Staged enforcement: Attempt 1% → 100% → verify rejected; must go 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%
  • Rollback speed: Set to 0% → verify all SSE clients notified within 3 seconds
  • Percentage accuracy: 10K users at 10% → verify ~10% ± 2% receive the feature
